ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked fish dish with spicy paste; no specific sambal pomfret composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for sambal pomfret. A0CKP (cooked fish) is used as the best defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sambal pomfret fish dish.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 1 medium piece of pomfret with sambal)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sambal pomfret fish is approximately 150 g, representing one medium-sized piece of pomfret coated with sambal paste, as commonly served in a Singaporean or Malaysian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Sambal Pomfret Fish — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sambal Pomfret Fish (cr_id F03020023), confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — Sambal Pomfret Fish is a predominantly protein-and-fat dish with negligible net digestible carbohydrate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sambal pomfret fish contains only 5.45 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 2.78 g is dietary fibre, yielding approximately 2.67 g net digestible carbohydrate per 100 g. At a 150 g serving, net digestible carbohydrate is approximately 4 g — too low for a meaningful GI or GL measurement. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for sambal pomfret or a sufficiently close Malay fish dish analogue. GI is set to 0 (sentinel for not-applicable in this numeric field) and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is set to 0.0 as the carbohydrate load is nutritionally negligible. The input record's non-approved 'Embedded GI Database' source has been replaced.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 758 mg per 100 g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; driven by belacan (shrimp paste), sambal paste, and salt marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid sambal pomfret fish due to its very high sodium content of 758 mg/100 g; if consumed, request reduced belacan and sambal paste, keep the portion to no more than 80–100 g, and offset with low-sodium foods throughout the day to stay within the 2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net digestible carbohydrate (~2.7 g/100 g after fibre), high protein (16 g/100 g), and moderate dietary fibre (2.78 g/100 g) result in a low glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal pomfret fish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 150 g serving, as its low net carbohydrate content and high protein slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response; however, the high fat content (17.3 g/100 g) should be factored into total daily energy intake, and steamed or grilled preparation is preferred over fried to minimise excess fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.27 g/100 g) and very high sodium (758 mg/100 g) are cardiovascular risk factors, partially offset by pomfret's omega-3 fatty acids and lean fish protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume sambal pomfret fish cautiously: the high sodium (758 mg/100 g) elevates blood pressure risk, and the total fat content (17.27 g/100 g) contributes to energy density; request steamed preparation with sambal on the side to reduce fat and sodium, limit portion to 100 g, and pair with high-potassium vegetables to partially counterbalance sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pomfret provides omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein with anti-inflammatory potential, but chilli (nightshade) in sambal and high sodium may aggravate inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from pomfret's omega-3 content, but should be aware that chilli and other nightshade ingredients in sambal paste may trigger joint flares in sensitive patients, and the high sodium (758 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue swelling; steamed preparation with reduced belacan is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Pomfret Fish (Ikan Bawal Sambal; 参巴鲳鱼) is a popular Malay and Singaporean seafood dish consisting of whole or portioned pomfret fish — typically white pomfret (Pampus argenteus) — that is steamed or pan-fried and then generously coated or served with a thick, aromatic sambal chilli paste. The sambal paste is made from a blend of dried chillies, shallots, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste), which is sautéed in oil until fragrant before being applied to the fish. The resulting dish has a bold, spicy, slightly pungent flavour with a tender, flaky fish texture beneath the rich, oily sambal coating. It is a staple at Malay hawker stalls, coffee shops, and home kitchens across Singapore and Malaysia, commonly served with steamed white rice and stir-fried vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 241 kcal, 16 g protein, 17.3 g fat, 5.5 g carbohydrate, 2.78 g dietary fibre, and 758 mg sodium. The high sodium content — driven by belacan and sambal paste — makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or those on sodium-restricted diets. The low net carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. The dish is halal and gluten-free. Pomfret is a white fish that provides some omega-3 fatty acids, contributing modest anti-inflammatory benefit. Individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced-belacan preparation and prefer steamed over fried cooking to minimise fat and sodium intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sambal pomfret fish?
Is sambal pomfret high in sodium?
Is sambal pomfret suitable for hypertension?
What fish is used in sambal pomfret?
Sambal pomfret fish calories per serving

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a reported confidence of 0.95 and provenance marked as canonical. The food identity is unambiguous. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 3.5 g/100 g based on typical pomfret and sambal composition; this field is the only estimated value. All other extended nutrient fields are set to -1 where the HPB record does not report them.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from typical white pomfret and sambal paste composition; the HPB Foodo record does not explicitly report saturated fat for this dish. This value should be updated when a direct HPB or USDA source reports it. 2. Fields set to -1 (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific sambal pomfret preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (cr_id F03020023) as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this composite Malay dish. 3. Glycemic index field is set to 0 (numeric sentinel) with gi_category 'not_applicable': sambal pomfret fish is a predominantly protein-and-fat dish with only approximately 2.67 g net digestible carbohydrate per 100 g, making GI measurement and GL calculation nutritionally meaningless. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for this dish or a sufficiently close analogue. The numeric value 0 is used here as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category 'not_applicable' is the authoritative classification. 4. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.316 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved GI source and has been replaced with the 0/not_applicable determination per q4 and the GI rules for low-carbohydrate foods. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate (closest_parent) matches only; no exact ontology codes exist for this composite Malay sambal fish dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per G5 validation feedback.

Iteration 3: Changed glycemic_index from null to numeric value 0 to satisfy q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document that 0 is used as a numeric sentinel for not-applicable. gi_category remains 'not_applicable' as the authoritative classification.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Sambal Pomfret Fish (F03020023) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. The primary fix in iteration 3 was changing glycemic_index from null to the numeric value 0 (sentinel) to satisfy the q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric, while retaining gi_category as 'not_applicable' to correctly classify this low-carbohydrate fish dish. All other sections remain compliant from previous iterations.
